hero.tagline: What runs the code - GPUs, real-time Linux, and the hardware foundation
hero.intro: Choosing your robot's compute is choosing your ceiling for everything else - perception throughput, control loop rate, ML model size, battery life. This branch covers the major options (NVIDIA Jetson, x86 NUCs, Raspberry Pi, Coral), when each makes sense, and the boring-but-critical concerns: real-time scheduling so a 1 kHz controller doesn't jitter, network latency, power and thermal budgets, ROS 2 QoS profiles, and observability for production deployments. The 'where do I run my code' chapter most courses skip.

sections.heading: The compute stack
sections.body: Most robots end up with two or three compute layers. A microcontroller (STM32, Teensy, RP2040) handles motor drivers, encoders, and the real-time control loop at 1+ kHz. A main computer (Jetson, NUC, Pi) runs ROS 2, perception, planning. Optionally an offboard or cloud machine handles training and heavy ML.
bullets: MCU layer - deterministic, fast, talks to motors and IMUs over UART/SPI/CAN
Onboard computer - Linux, ROS 2, perception + planning + behaviors
Offboard / cloud - RL training, ML inference for non-real-time tasks, fleet management
Bridge between layers: micro-ROS, rosserial, custom serial protocols

sections.heading: Embedded compute - Jetson Thor, Orin, Pi, Coral
sections.body: For robots that have to fit on the platform and run on battery, the choice is usually between NVIDIA Jetson (GPU + ARM CPU, expensive but unbeatable for vision/ML), Raspberry Pi (cheap, great community, no GPU acceleration for ML), and accelerator add-ons like Google Coral (TPU, fast but model-restricted). For 2026 humanoid + AV workloads, NVIDIA's Jetson Thor (released 2025) is the new top of the line - 2,000 TFLOPS FP4, 128 GB shared memory, designed specifically to run VLAs like GR00T and full BEV perception on-device.
bullets: Jetson Thor (~$3,500) - 2,000 TFLOPS FP4, 128 GB unified, 130 W. The 2026 humanoid / AV brain. Runs GR00T, Cosmos Policy, full BEV perception
Jetson AGX Orin (~$2,000) - 275 TOPS, laptop-class. Still the most-deployed compute on serious mobile robots in 2026
Jetson Orin NX (~$900) / Orin Nano (~$500) - 100 / 40 TOPS, great for sub-10kg AMRs, drones, dev kits
Raspberry Pi 5 (~$80) - great for control, light perception via Coral USB stick
Coral USB / M.2 - 4 TOPS INT8, runs quantized TFLite models only
Power draw: Thor at 130 W needs serious cooling. AGX Orin pulls 60 W under load. Orin Nano stays under 15 W

sections.heading: x86 mini-PCs and NUCs
sections.body: When the robot has the size and power for it, an x86 mini-PC (Intel NUC, ASUS PN, Beelink, Minisforum) crushes ARM SBCs on raw compute, has predictable performance, and runs every Linux package without ARM gotchas. A modern NUC with i7 + 32 GB + NVMe is around $700 and handles ROS 2 + perception + planning effortlessly.
bullets: Pros: x86 software ecosystem, more RAM, real PCIe lanes (M.2 NVMe, sometimes a discrete GPU)
Cons: usually heavier, higher power, often no GPU (unless you go gaming-laptop sized)
Spec tip: minimum 16 GB RAM, NVMe SSD, 4+ cores. Get USB-C PD if you'll battery-power it
Watch the TDP - a 65W i9 in a fanless case will thermally throttle

sections.heading: GPUs - when you need them, what to buy
sections.body: If you're running learned perception (YOLO, SAM, SLAM with deep features), a VLM, or training in sim, you need a GPU. The decision is integrated (Jetson) vs discrete (laptop / desktop NVIDIA card).
bullets: Inference only, on-robot - Jetson Orin Nano covers small models; AGX for VLMs
Training - desktop with RTX 4090 / 5090, or rent A100 / H100 by the hour on Lambda / RunPod
VRAM is the binding constraint for VLMs / VLAs - OpenVLA-7B needs ~16 GB FP16
Avoid AMD GPUs for robotics ML - CUDA ecosystem is still dominant in 2026

sections.heading: Real-time control + microcontrollers
sections.body: For control loops faster than ~200 Hz with hard timing requirements, run them on a microcontroller, not Linux. The MCU talks to the main computer over a serial bus. Modern options:
bullets: STM32 (F4 / H7 / G4) - the industry workhorse for motor control + sensors
Teensy 4.x - Arduino-friendly, very fast (Cortex-M7 at 600 MHz)
RP2040 / RP2350 (Pi Pico) - cheap, two cores, programmable IO is amazing for encoders
ESP32-S3 - has Wi-Fi + BT for wireless robots, less rigorous timing than STM32
micro-ROS bridges any of these to ROS 2 over serial / Ethernet / CAN

codeBlocks.title: Add micro-ROS support to a Raspberry Pi Pico project
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src
git clone -b jazzy https://github.com/micro-ROS/micro_ros_setup.git
cd ~/{{ws}}
rosdep install --from-paths src --ignore-src -r -y
colcon build
source install/setup.bash
ros2 run micro_ros_setup create_firmware_ws.sh host

sections.heading: Networking, DDS, and latency
sections.body: ROS 2 uses DDS for transport. By default, every node on every machine on the same subnet discovers every other node - convenient, but it can saturate the network with discovery traffic on big robots. Tune it with ROS_DOMAIN_ID, FastDDS or Cyclone DDS profiles, and Discovery Server on bigger systems.
bullets: Wired Gigabit ethernet beats Wi-Fi for any non-trivial topic bandwidth
Wi-Fi 6 / 6E if wireless - and pick a band with no other traffic, ideally a dedicated AP
ROS_DOMAIN_ID isolates groups of nodes so robots don't talk to each other
Cyclone DDS is the more predictable default for Humble; FastDDS is the actual default in some distros
Big topics (images, point clouds) - use image_transport / point_cloud_transport with compressed plugins

codeBlocks.title: Switch to Cyclone DDS
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-rmw-cyclonedds-cpp
export RMW_IMPLEMENTATION=rmw_cyclonedds_cpp
export ROS_DOMAIN_ID=42  # any 0-101

sections.heading: Power and thermal budgets
sections.body: On battery-powered robots, every watt matters. Build the budget early: Jetson AGX (60 W) + LiDAR (8 W) + 4× cameras (10 W) + 6 motors at full load (200+ W) adds up fast. Battery: Wh = Ah × V. A 20 Ah 24 V LiPo is 480 Wh - about an hour of moderate driving on a 50 cm robot. For thermal, anything in a sealed enclosure needs forced air or a heatsink + spreader; passively cooled compute throttles within minutes under sustained ML load.
bullets: Always reserve 20% battery for safe-return / shutdown
BMS (battery management system) is non-negotiable on Li chemistry - get one with cell balancing
Jetsons have selectable power modes (nvpmodel) - run the lowest mode that meets your perf
Active cooling on compute, passive on motors (until they need fans too)
Estimate before you buy - many robots get redesigned because the battery wouldn't fit

codeBlocks.title: Jetson - pick a power mode (max perf vs battery saving)
codeBlocks.code:
sudo nvpmodel -q                 # show current mode
sudo nvpmodel -m 0               # MAXN - uncapped, hottest, fastest
sudo nvpmodel -m 1               # 15 W mode for Orin Nano
sudo jetson_clocks               # lock all clocks to max within the mode

codeBlocks.title: tegrastats - live SoC + GPU + temp + power
codeBlocks.code:
sudo tegrastats --interval 500
# RAM 1234/8048MB ... GR3D_FREQ 67%@624 ... CPU [80%@1920,...]
#                ... AO@36C GPU@40C ... POM_5V_IN 4567/4321

codeBlocks.title: Read battery voltage on a UPS HAT (INA219 over I2C)
codeBlocks.code:
from smbus2 import SMBus
bus = SMBus(1)
ADDR = 0x42
# INA219 register 2 = bus voltage; raw / 4 = mV (LSB = 4 mV)
raw = bus.read_word_data(ADDR, 0x02)
msb, lsb = (raw >> 8) & 0xff, raw & 0xff
bus_v = (((lsb << 8) | msb) >> 3) * 0.004   # volts
print(f'Battery: {bus_v:.2f} V')

sections.heading: Logging, telemetry & observability
sections.body: On a real robot, half your time is spent figuring out what went wrong. Build observability in early. The minimum: structured logs (rclpy logger), bag recordings of key topics, and a metrics dashboard. Beyond the minimum: distributed tracing across nodes, anomaly detection on bag streams, automatic incident artifacts when the robot e-stops.
bullets: ros2 bag - record everything you might want to see again. Storage is cheap, repro is priceless
MCAP - modern bag format, plays back in Foxglove Studio, much faster than rosbag2 sqlite3
rqt_graph + rqt_console - quick during dev; doesn't scale to production fleets
Grafana + Prometheus - system metrics (CPU, RAM, GPU temp, battery) over time
Sentry / OpenTelemetry - exception tracking and distributed tracing across nodes
Always include topic stats: ros2 topic hz / bw - silent topic = dead sensor

codeBlocks.title: Black-box bag - last 60 s of critical topics, rotating
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 bag record -o /var/log/blackbox \
    --max-bag-duration 60 --max-bag-size 268435456 \
    /tf /tf_static /odom /scan /cmd_vel /robot_status \
    --storage mcap --compression-mode file --compression-format zstd

codeBlocks.title: Convert old rosbag2 sqlite bags to MCAP
codeBlocks.code:
ros2 bag convert -i old_bag/old_bag_0.db3 in:sqlite3 \
    -o new_bag --storage mcap

codeBlocks.title: Custom metric publisher feeding Prometheus
codeBlocks.code:
from prometheus_client import start_http_server, Gauge
import psutil, rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node

CPU = Gauge('robot_cpu_percent', 'CPU usage')
MEM = Gauge('robot_mem_mb', 'Memory used MB')
BAT = Gauge('robot_battery_v', 'Battery voltage')

class Metrics(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('metrics')
        start_http_server(9100)
        self.create_timer(1.0, self.tick)
    def tick(self):
        CPU.set(psutil.cpu_percent())
        MEM.set(psutil.virtual_memory().used / 1e6)
        # Update BAT from your battery topic subscription

sections.heading: QoS - getting the right delivery guarantees
sections.body: ROS 2 DDS lets every publisher and subscriber pick its own QoS profile - reliability (BEST_EFFORT vs RELIABLE), durability (VOLATILE vs TRANSIENT_LOCAL), history (KEEP_LAST vs KEEP_ALL), depth (queue size). Mismatched QoS = subscriber never receives. The defaults rarely fit production: sensor topics want BEST_EFFORT, commands want RELIABLE, latched topics need TRANSIENT_LOCAL.
bullets: BEST_EFFORT - fire and forget; right for sensor streams (image, scan, IMU)
RELIABLE - retransmit until ACK; right for commands, parameters, lifecycle
TRANSIENT_LOCAL - late-joining subscribers get the last N messages (latched)
KEEP_ALL - never drop; bounded only by RAM. Use carefully
Deadline + lifespan - safety-relevant topics; declare expected rate
Liveliness - heartbeat semantics; detect dead publishers fast

codeBlocks.title: Sensor publisher with explicit QoS
codeBlocks.code:
from rclpy.qos import QoSProfile, ReliabilityPolicy, DurabilityPolicy, HistoryPolicy

sensor_qos = QoSProfile(
    reliability=ReliabilityPolicy.BEST_EFFORT,
    durability=DurabilityPolicy.VOLATILE,
    history=HistoryPolicy.KEEP_LAST,
    depth=5
)
self.pub = self.create_publisher(LaserScan, '/scan', sensor_qos)

sections.heading: Composition + lifecycle for production stacks
sections.body: Every ROS 2 node in its own process is convenient for development but wasteful for production - every topic between two nodes serializes, copies, and IPC-hops. Composition packs many "components" into one process so they can share memory directly via intra-process zero-copy. Lifecycle nodes give you deterministic startup / shutdown so your stack can recover from a sensor reboot without a full restart.
bullets: Component - a class that registers with a ComponentContainer instead of running its own main()
Intra-process comms: zero-copy when both pub and sub live in the same container
Container choice: rclcpp_components::ComponentManager for general use
Lifecycle: unconfigured → inactive → active → inactive → finalized
Activate / deactivate hooks: open / close hardware connections cleanly
Use launch_ros.actions.LifecycleNode + register_event_handler for orchestration

codeBlocks.title: Register a node as a composable component
